critique.summary: Pyodide is a Python distribution that runs in web browsers and Node.js environments using WebAssembly. It ports CPython to WebAssembly, enabling the use of Python packages, including those with C, C++, and Rust extensions, directly in the browser with a robust JavaScript-Python interface.
mainPoints: Pyodide is a port of CPython to WebAssembly/Emscripten, allowing Python to run in browsers and Node.js.
It supports installing pure Python packages from PyPi via micropip and includes many popular packages with native extensions like NumPy, pandas, SciPy, and Matplotlib.
Pyodide provides a comprehensive JavaScript-Python foreign function interface, supporting features like error handling and async/await.
Python code running in the browser via Pyodide has full access to Web APIs.
The project originated in 2018 at Mozilla as part of the Iodide project and is now a community-driven open-source project.
Pyodide is licensed under the Mozilla Public License 2.0 and has a large contributor base and active sponsorship.
critique.truthfulness: The claims in the article are accurate and consistent with the official Pyodide GitHub repository and documentation. Pyodide is indeed a WebAssembly-based Python distribution that supports many Python packages and provides a JavaScript-Python interoperability layer. The historical context and licensing information align with publicly available sources. The description of features and supported packages matches the official project details, confirming the article's reliability.
considerations: Performance limitations of running Python in the browser compared to native execution are not discussed.
Security implications of running Python code and packages in the browser environment are not mentioned.
The article does not address browser compatibility or potential limitations in different environments.
No mention is made of the size of the Pyodide distribution and its impact on load times in web applications.
The article does not compare Pyodide with alternative solutions for running Python in the browser.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion highlights Pyodide as a mature and versatile Python distribution for WebAssembly, praised for enabling Python execution in browsers and integration with various tools. Users share practical applications, performance considerations, and related projects, with some expressing hopes for similar tools in other languages.

comments.author: simonw
comments.text:
Pyodide is one of the hidden gems of the Python ecosystem. It's SO good at what it does, and it's nearly 8 years old now so it's pretty mature.

I love using Pyodide to build web UIs for trying out new Python libraries. Here's one I built a few weeks ago to exercise my pure-Python SQLite AST parser, for example: https://tools.simonwillison.net/sqlite-ast

It's also pretty easy[1] to get C or Rust libraries that have Python bindings compiled to a WebAssembly wheel that Pyodide can then load.

Here's a bit of a nutty example - the new Monty Python-like sandbox library (written in Rust) compiled to WASM and then loaded in Pyodide in the browser: https://simonw.github.io/research/monty-wasm-pyodide/pyodide...

[1] OK, Claude Code knows how to do it.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed and accurate overview of Pyodide's maturity and capabilities, supported by examples and links, making it highly reliable and informative.

comments.author: fzumstein
comments.text: Pyodide powers xlwings Lite, a free Excel add-in that you can install from Excel’s add-in store with a single click. It outperforms Microsoft’s official Python in Excel solution in every coceivable way: price, privacy, speed, can install packages, can access the internet, can access local files, doesn’t have a usage quota, can automate Excel and create native UDFs. See https://lite.xlwings.org
comments.analysis: The comment accurately describes a real-world application of Pyodide with specific advantages, contributing valuable insight into its practical use.

comments.author: devsda
comments.text:
There's also xeus-cpp which is cpp based on wasm. Xeus-cpp and pyodide are the backends for Jupyterlite [1] kernels in browser.

It's actually a very good way to teach coding in python/c++(11, 17 or even 23), explore language features or your own library within browser and allow students to execute code by hosting just some static html pages and some assets without any backend.

1. https://jupyter.org/try-jupyter/lab/
comments.analysis: This comment correctly identifies related technologies and their educational benefits, providing useful context about the ecosystem around Pyodide.

comments.author: mcintyre1994
comments.text:
Love all the tools mentioned in the comments, Pyodide is a fun tool. I built https://pyground.vercel.app with it a few years ago, as a quick way to run Python on a CSV/JSON file locally in the browser. For a while Python/Matplotlib was what I was most comfortable writing quick and simple analysis scripts with, and this was my go to for one-off data analysis work. You can drag-drop a file in, then some 'clever' code makes it available as `data` in the Python script so you don't have to write any parsing code yourself.

Now I'd probably just get Claude Code to write something locally, but there might be some value in a version of pyground that can use an LLM to write the Python code for you (and give it the data shape) but keeps the local execution setup the same.
comments.analysis: The comment shares a personal project using Pyodide, illustrating practical uses and potential future enhancements involving LLMs, which is plausible and constructive.

comments.author: QuadmasterXLII
comments.text: It works surprisingly well in terms of writing python for native execution, and then trying to share on the web and having it Just Work TM. Unfortunately, when I want python it's because I want numpy, scipy and friends, and once you bring them on board pyodide load times are long. As an example comparison: a face turning octahedron puzzle in python + pyodide, loads in 10 seconds https://cubes.hgreer.com/fto.html . Meanwhile, a megaminx puzzle in javascript (statically generated by python) loads in 200 ms https://cubes.hgreer.com/ssg/output.html
comments.analysis: This comment provides a realistic performance critique based on experience, highlighting a known limitation of Pyodide with scientific libraries.

comments.author: screamingninja
comments.text:
https://marimo.app/

A reactive notebook for Python that runs in browser, powered by Pyodide. I have been using it extensively for teaching Python to non-STEM students that do not need the added friction of navigating the CLI or filesystem hierarchy.
comments.analysis: The comment introduces a relevant project leveraging Pyodide for educational purposes, adding useful information without exaggeration.

comments.author: ianberdin
comments.text:
Yea, I even built online ide based on Pyodide. Insane peace of software. 
You can try here: playcode.io/python
comments.analysis: While brief and enthusiastic, the comment is credible as it references a personal project demonstrating Pyodide's capabilities.

comments.author: jasiek
comments.text: I've used it to put together a browser app to program walkie-talkies: https://codeplug.org
comments.analysis: This comment shares a niche but plausible application of Pyodide, contributing to the diversity of use cases.

comments.author: yjftsjthsd-h
comments.text: That is very cool. Has anyone made a nice beginner's learning environment out of it? Seems like it would solve some of the extra friction that makes it hard to get started.
comments.analysis: The comment raises a valid question about educational tooling but does not provide concrete information or examples.

comments.author: yawnxyz
comments.text: no but it works great in conjunction with observable and d3js for pretty interactives!
comments.analysis: This comment adds a useful note about combining Pyodide with other visualization tools, though it is brief and somewhat vague.

comments.author: jacob019
comments.text: Anyone using it with nodejs to make a sandbox for code agents?
comments.analysis: This is a speculative inquiry rather than an informative comment, contributing little factual content.

comments.author: williamstein
comments.text: I tried to make a version of this using Zig once, but ran out of steam: https://cowasm.org/
comments.analysis: The comment is a personal anecdote about a related project attempt, adding minor context but no evaluation of Pyodide.

comments.author: Archit3ch
comments.analysis: This comment expresses a wish for a similar tool in another language but does not provide factual information or analysis.

comments.author: shevy-java
comments.text: WebAssembly will reach a breakthrough in the same year as GNU Hurd will dominate, together with the Desktop-Linux-of-the-year.
comments.analysis: This comment is a sarcastic or hyperbolic statement with little basis in reality, reducing its credibility.
